Facts.text: Marcellus I begins his reign as Catholic Pope, serving until his death in 309
Greek scholar and new Archbishop of Canterbury, Theodore of Tarsus arrives in Canterbury with North African abbot Hadrian
Battle of the Bosnian Highlands: Simeon I of Bulgaria is defeated by King Tomislav of Croatia
Richard III of Capua is anointed as prince two weeks before his untimely death
Guy of Dampierre, Count of Flanders, takes financial responsibility for Bruges
30 Jews of Posing, Hungary, charged with blood ritual, burned at stake
Battle of Shanhai Pass: Li Zicheng's army defeated by combined Ming and Manchu force
Denmark and Sweden sign the Treaty of Copenhagen, ending the Second Northern War
Habeas Corpus Act passes in England, strengthening a person's right to challenge unlawful arrest and imprisonment
Anthonie Heinsius succeeds G. Fagel as Grand Pensionary of Holland
Turkish troops occupy Orsova and Ochakov in eastern Europe
James S. McLean granted a US patent for his improvements to the piano
The Battle of Oulart Hill takes place in Wexford, Ireland, large rebel force kills local militia
Americans capture Fort George, Canada
Mormon Temple in Nauvoo, Illinois, destroyed by tornado
Marine Telegraph from Fort Point to San Francisco completed
Doctor William Palmer (the Rugeley Poisoner) found guilty of poisoning in Stafford, England
Battle of Hanover Court House, Virginia (Slash Church, Peake's Station)
US Civil War: Confederate gunboat CSS Chattahoochee steam boiler explosion kills 19 crew members on the Apalachicola River, near Blountstown, Florida
US Civil War: Siege of Port Hudson in Louisiana by Union forces begins; lasts 48 days, longest American military siege
Skirmish at Salem Church (Haw's Shop), Virginia
Audath Yisroel forms at Kattowitz (Katowice) Poland
British inventor Birt Acres patents film camera/projector
Bay District Race Track in San Francisco closes
Tornado hit St Louis, killing 255, leaving thousands homeless
Arthur Pinero's "Trelawney of the Wells" premieres in London
Lord Roberts' army fights the Vaal in South Africa
NY Giants first baseman Dan McGann collects MLB record 5 stolen bases in 3-1 win over the Brooklyn Superbas at the Polo Grounds in NYC; record broken in 1991 by Otis Nixon
Bubonic Plague breaks out in San Francisco
Maulana Hakeem Noor-ud-Din iss elected the first Khalifa of Ahmadiyya Muslim Community.
Reform of Prussian three-class voting system fails
Groundbreaking begins on Hugh J. Grant Circle, a park in the Bronx, named for the youngest mayor of New York City (1899-92)
Third Battle of Aisne: German offensive overcomes British forces (WWI)
First transatlantic flight ends after an 11-day journey by a US Navy flying boat
Tatar ASSR forms in Russian SFSR
Japanese military intervenes in Chinese civil war in Shantung to protect Japanese residents there
Richard Drew invents masking tape
First full-scale wind tunnel for testing airplanes at Langley Field, Virginia
Swiss Auguste Piccard and Paul Kipfer make 1st flight into stratosphere, by balloon from Augsburg, Germany
Austrian communist party banned
Century of Progress Exposition opens in Chicago
Federal Securities Act signed
Trailing 11-3, Yanks score 12 runs in 8th & beat White Sox 15-11
Walt Disney's short film "3 Little Pigs" released (Academy Award Best Animated film 1934)
First flight of Irish airline Aer Lingus, a six-seater de Havilland DH.84 Dragon biplane between Baldonnel Airfield in Clondalkin, County Dublin and Bristol (Whitchurch) Airport, England
RMS Queen Mary leaves Southampton for NY on maiden voyage
Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco opens to pedestrians
World War II: In the Le Paradis massacre, 97 soldiers from a Royal Norfolk Regiment unit are shot after surrendering to German troops
FDRdeclares state of emergency after a German U-boat sinks the American flagged SS Robin Moor
German battleship Bismarck sunk by British naval force
Dorie Miller awarded navy cross for deeds at Pearl Harbor
Italian army begin siege of French western Fort Bir Hachim
French resistance under Jean Moulin meets secretly in Paris
Allies land on Biak, Indonesia (operation Horlicks)
Japanese advance in Hangkhou, China
Arab League of Jordan forces blow up Hurvat Rabbi Yehudah he-Hasid synagogue in Jerusalem, after 2 days of threats
Hank Greenbergbuys an interest in the Cleveland Indians baseball team
Martin Canine, cartoon character, spoofs Martin Kane
MLB Cleveland Indians start 12-17, ownerBill Veeckarranges a "Second Opening Day"
Bollingen Prize for poetry awarded to Wallace Stevens
European Defense Community forms
Dutch social democratic/Dutch Liberal Party win municipal elections
"The Red Buttons Show", last airs on NBC-TV
Boston Red Sox first baseman Norm Zauchin accumulates 10 RBIs in 16-0 win over Washington Senators
French raid in Algiers
US performs nuclear test at Enewetak Atoll (atmospheric tests)
Toronto's CHUM-AM, (1050 kHz) becomes Canada's first radio station to broadcast only top 40 Rock n' Roll music format
Maiden flight of the F-4 Phantom II
Vanguard SLV-1 launched for Earth orbit (failed)
Baltimore manager Paul Richards devises oversized catcher's mitt, used by Baltimore Oriole Clint Courtney
Military coup overthrows democratic government of Turkey
1st black light is sold
Fiorentina of Italy wins the first European Cup Winners' Cup against Glasgow Rangers 4-2 in Florence (second leg)
55th German F-104 Starfighter crashes
Six French fighter jets crash over Spain
Australians vote in favor of a constitutional referendum granting the Australian government the power to make laws to benefit Indigenous Australians and count them in the national census
British folk-jazz fusion group Pentangle gives debut performance at the Royal Festival Hall, London, England
Meeting of the Union Nationale des Étudiants de France (National Union of the Students of France) takes place. 30,000 to 50,000 people gather in the Stade Sebastien Charlety
NL awards Montreal and San Diego major league franchises
Walt Disney World construction begins at Bay Lake and Lake Buena Vista, Florida
British expedition climbs south face of Annapurna I in the Himalayas in north-central Nepal, 10th highest mountain
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Jimmy Castor Bunch's "Troglodyte (Cave Man)" hits #6
American athlete Rick Wohlhuter runs world record 880 yards in 1:44.6

Stanley Cup Final, The Aud, Buffalo, NY: Philadelphia Flyers win back-to-back titles; shutting out Buffalo Sabres, 2-0 for a 4-2 series win; goaltender Bernie Parent wins 2nd consecutive Conn Smyth trophy as playoff MVP

Worst motor vehicle disaster in UK; brakes fail on a bus full of elderly women and it plunges 17 feet off the road at Dibble's Bridge, Yorkshire, killing 33; a similar accident occurred there killing 11 in 1925
30th Cannes Film Festival: "Padre Padrone" directed by Paolo and Vittorio Taviani wins the Palme d'Or
South Korean police ends people's uprising; 2,000 killed
The Gwangju Massacre: South Korean airborne and army troops retake city of Gwangju from civil militias, killing at least 207 possibly many more
34th Cannes Film Festival: "Man of Iron" directed by Andrzej Wajda wins the Palme d'Or
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
New owners headed by John McMullen buy NHL's Colorado Rockies and move the team to the New Jersey Meadowlands under the new name NJ Devils
Former EPA official Rita Lavelle indicted for contempt of Congress
Beth Henley's "Miss Firecracker contest" premieres in NYC
Britain agrees to return Hong Kong to China in 1997
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Norway Showcase groundbreaking
President Reaganorders two Poseidon-class submarines to be dismantled
Christian evangelists Jim and Tammy Bakker appear on "Nightline" after PTL scandal
Senate ratified a treaty eliminating medium-range nuclear missiles
Radical Democratic Party holds 1st political meetings in Moscow
Austrian Boeing 767-300 explodes at Bangkok, 223 die
Mafia bombs Uffizi Museum in Florence, killing 6
"La Haine", directed by Mathieu Kassovitz and starring Vincent Cassel, premieres at the Cannes Film Festival in France
First all-female team of 20 British women reaches the North Pole
Judge finds Pamela Lee not guilty of breaking a contract
Major league revenue sharing begins, NY Yanks pay out most $28M
Michael Fortier is sentenced to 12 years in prison and fined $200,000 for failing to warn authorities about the plot to bomb Oklahoma City
Super Rugby Final, Canberra: Crusaders claim their 3rd consecutive title with a 20-19 win over the ACT Brumbies; flyhalf Andrew Mehrtens kicks 5 penalties for the winners
An earthquake strikes Java, Indonesia, at 5:53:58 AM local time, devastating Bantul and the city of Yogyakarta and killing over 6,600 people
60th Cannes Film Festival: "4 Months, 3 Weeks and 2 Days" directed by Cristian Mungiu wins the Palme d'Or
South Africa enters the global recession; the first recession for South Africa in 17 years
65th Cannes Film Festival: "Amour" directed by Michael Haneke wins the Palme d'Or
A NATO airstrike in Afghanistan kills a family of eight, including six children
75 people are killed and 200 are injured in a wave of bombings across Iraq
The largest flag ever made at 5 tons with 44 miles of thread is unveiled in Romania
Golden State Warriors win the NBA Western Conference
Three ships in three days sink while carrying immigrants across the Mediterranean, drowning over 700 people
In Bangalore, India, white puffy toxic foam begins spilling out of Varthur Lake onto city streets
Oil workers for Brazilian state oil company Petrobrás join the truckers' national strike
European Parliament elections result in centralist parties losing their majority with liberals, the Greens and nationalists picking up more votes
World's rivers widely contaminated with antibiotics according to new global study of 711 sites
America's COVID-19 death toll passes 100,000 (Johns Hopkins figures) equal to number of US servicemen and women killed in Korea, Vietnam, Iraq and Afghanistan put together
Locust swarms in western and central India worst since 1993 after spreading from Pakistan and Iran and due to extreme weather
Spain begins 10 days of mourning for victims of COVID-19 with death toll just under 27,000
English playwright William Shakespeare is reported to have died after receiving the COVID-19 vaccine by Argentine news channel Canal 26 (case of mistaken identity, the Bard died in 1616)
Rhiannon Giddens and Michael Abels' opera "Omar" premieres at the Spoleto Festival USA in Charleston, South Carolina (2023 Pulitzer Prize for Music winner)
76th Cannes Film Festival: French director Justine Triet's "Anatomy of a Fall" wins the Palme d'Or
Texas House of Representatives votes to impeach Republican Attorney General Ken Paxton - just the third official in Texan history

US National Park Service quietly removes all references to bisexual people from Stonewall National Monument, further erasing LGBTQ+ history following the removal of references to transgender and queer people in February
John crowned King of England after the death of his brotherRichard I
Saint Petersburg (Leningrad) is founded by Russian TsarPeter the Great
Heinrich Schliemanndiscovers "Priam's Treasure" a cache of gold and other objects in Hisarlik (Troy) in Anatolia
Alexander IIIcrowned Tsar of Russia in Moscow
Queen Wilhelminaopens the new Amsterdam stock exchange Beurs van Berlages, designed by influential architect Hendrik Petrus Berlage
First outlining ofGustav Mahler's 6th Symphony in Essen, Germany
PresidentWoodrow Wilsonaddresses the League to Enforce Peace, founded in 1915, and gives public support to the idea of a league of nations
Tomáš Masaryk(77) is elected President of Czechoslovakia, for the second time
The 1,046-foot (319-meter)ChryslerBuilding in New York City, the tallest man-made structure at the time, opens to the public
Martin Heideggergives his inaugural rectoral address on “The Self-Assertion of the German University”, taken by some take as supporting the Nazi regime
Supreme Court declaresFDR's National Recovery Act unconstitutional
New York Giants pitcherCarl Hubbellwins his MLB record 24th consecutive game in a 3-2 win over the Cincinnati Reds at Crosley Field; streak starts July 17, 1936

Czech rebels attempt assassination of Nazi leaderReinhard Heydrichby grenade in Prague during Operation Anthropoid; he dies a week later from an infection while recovering from his injuries.Adolf Hitlerorders 10,000 Czechs murdered in revenge, leading to the annihilation of the village of Lidice in June
Jean-Paul Sartre's existentialist  play "Huis Clos" (No Exit) premieres in Paris
Chinese Communists forceDalai Lamato surrender his army to Beijing
Ralph Bostonof US, sets then long jump record at 27' ½"

Columbia Records releases "The Freewheelin'Bob Dylan", the second studio album by folk singer Bob Dylan; it contains his songs "Blowin' in the Wind", "A Hard Rain's a-Gonna Fall" and "Don't Think Twice, It's All Right", and serves as his critical and commercial breakthrough
Jomo Kenyattais elected as the first Prime Minister of Kenya
George Halasretires from coaching, finishing with 318 regular-season wins and 6 NFL titles
Jerry LewisShow second run, last airs on NBC-TV
24th Cannes Film Festival: "The Go-Between" directed by Joseph Losey wins the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
John Lennonrecords the song "Imagine" at his Ascot Sound home studio at Tittenhurst Park, England
PiratesKen Brettno-hits Padres until 9th inning
The Sex Pistolsrelease "God Save the Queen," sparking major controversy and leading to a ban on the song by the BBC
PopeJohn Paul IIordains John J O'Connor as a bishop

Manuela Maleeva of Bulgaria wins 3 singles tennis matches in one day; Italian Open 1984, beats Virginie Ruzici, Carling Basset andChris Evertin the final 6-3 6-3; only 7th player to beat Evert on clay
Inaugural bands parade forPresident Reagan
New York Yankees knuckleballerPhil Niekrois 3rd pitcher to make 700th start, joining Cy Young and Don Sutton
Baseball playerDale Murphyends his career after 398 HRs
"The Flintstones" live action movie based in the 1960s cartoon, starringJohn Goodman(as Fred),Rick Moranis(as Barney), andRosie O'Donnell(as Betty) premieres
Final broadcast of TV talk show "TheArsenio HallShow"
Larry Kingends his radio show
WriterAleksandr Solzhenitsynreturns to Russia after 20 years in exile
ActorChristopher Reeveis paralyzed from the neck down after falling from his horse in a riding competition in Culpeper, Virginia
Russian PresidentBoris Yeltsinsigns a historic treaty with NATO
International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia indictsSlobodan Miloševićand four others for war crimes and crimes against humanity committed in Kosovo
DreamWorks releases computer-animated film "Madagascar" with voices byBen Stiller,Chris Rock,David Schwimmer, andJada Pinkett-Smith
"Pan's Labyrinth" fantasy film on the Spanish Civil War written and directed byGuillermo del Torodebuts at Cannes
Super Rugby Final, Christchurch: Canterbury Crusaders retain title with a 19-12 win over the Wellington Hurricanes;Dan Carterkicks 4 penalties & a conversion for the winners
Andrew Scottwins his first BAFTA Award for Best Supporting Actor for the role of Moriarty in BBC's "Sherlock"
The director of the International Monetary Fund (IMF)Christine Lagardecalls for "tougher regulation and tighter supervision" of banking sector
Italian PM-designateGiuseppe Contegives up attempt to form coalition government after President Sergio Matarella vetoes his choice of economy minister
South Korean boy bandBTSare the first K-pop group to top the US Billboard 200 with their album "Love Yourself: Tear"
Austrian ChancellorSebastian Kurzremoved from office after a no-confidence vote in parliament, Vice Chancellor Hartwig Löger appointed interim chancellor
US Secretary of StateMike Pompeosays Hong Kong no longer has autonomy from China, doesn't merit special trade relationship, in note to Congress
"Friends" reunion screens, 17 years after the TV sitcom series ended
US PresidentJoe Bidencalls for a ceasefire in the Tigray conflict, north Ethiopia, citing killings and "widespread sexual violence" as a weapon of war
"Top Gun Maverick" the sequel starringTom Cruise, Miles Teller andJennifer Connellyis released 36 years after the original film
Ringo Starr's fifteenth All-Starr Band debuts in concert; members include: Steve Lukather, Edgar Winter, Colin Hay, Hamish Stuart, Gregg Bissonette, and Warren Ham
Vice PresidentKamala Harrisbecomes 1st woman to deliver commencement address at US Military Academy graduation ceremony in West Point, New York
William Phips, Governor of Massachusetts Bay Colony authorizes a special Court of Oyer and Terminer ("to hear and determine"), to administer Salem witch trials
Opening of the Great Hall at Euston station in London
Japanese fleet destroys the Russian East Sea fleet in the Battle of Tsushima, the only decisive clash between modern steel battleships in history
British and Allied forces begin the evacuation of Dunkirk (Operation Dynamo) during World War II
Ernest Green becomes the 1st African-American to graduate from Little Rock's Central High School
John Hinckley, Jr attempts suicide by overdosing on Tylenol pain relief medication
French PresidentEmmanuel Macronrecognizes France's role in the 1994 Rwandan Genocide after a meeting with Rwandan President Paul Kagame in Kigali, Rwanda
Girolamo Mei, Italian historian and editor of Greek texts, born in Florence (d. 1594)
Caspar Schoppe, German scholar and controversialist, born in Neumarkt in der Oberpfalz, Germany (d. 1649)
Benedikt Carpzov Jr., German criminal lawyer and witchcraft theoretician, born in Wittenberg, Germany (d. 1666)
Antoine Daniel, French Jesuit missionary and martyr, born in Dieppe, Normandie, France (d. 1648)
William II, Prince of Orange and stadtholder of the United Provinces of the Netherlands, born in The Hague, Dutch Republic (d. 1650)
Louis-Antoine, Cardinal de Noailles, French bishop and cardinal, born in Auvergne, France (d. 1729)
Elisabeth Charlotte "Liselotte", German-French duchess of Orleans, born in Heidelberg Castle, Heidelberg, Electoral Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1722)
Bonaventura Furlanetto, Italian composer, born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1817)
Nathaniel Gorham, American politician (6th President of the Confederation Congress), born in Charlestown, Massachusetts (d. 1796)
Maximilian I Joseph, King of Bavaria, born in Schwetzingen, Baden (d. 1825)
Francis Beaufort, Irish admiral and hydrographer (Beaufort wind force scale), born in Navan, County Meath, Ireland (d. 1857)
Fromental Halévy, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1862)
Charles-Joseph Tolbecque, French violinist, and composer, born in Paris (d. 1835)
Henry Parkes, Australian politician (longest non-consecutive Premier of the Colony of New South Wales), born in Canley, Warwickshire, England (d. 1896)
Amelia Jenks Bloomer, American reformer who campaigned for temperance and women’s rights, born in Homer, New York (d. 1894)
Julia Ward Howe, American poet (Battle Hymn of the Republic), author, abolitionist, and women's suffragist, born in New York City (d. 1910)

Mathilde Bonaparte, French princess (daughter of Napoleon's brother Jérôme Bonaparte and his second wife, Catharina of Württemberg, daughter of King Frederick I of Württemberg) and socialite, born in Trieste, Austrian Empire (d. 1904)
Henry Wylde, English composer and conductor, born in Bushey, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1890)
Joachim Raff, German-Swiss composer, born in Lachen, Switzerland (d. 1882)
John Gray Foster, American Major General (Union Army), born in Whitefield, New Hampshire (d. 1874)
Samuel F. Miller, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from New York), born in Franklin, New York (d. 1892)
Edwin Gray Lee, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Shepherdstown, Virginia (d. 1870)
Jay Gould, American railroad developer and speculator, born in Roxbury, New York (d. 1892)
Ivan Kramskoi, Russian painter and art critic, born in Ostrogozhsk, Voronezh Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1887)
Robert Hoke, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Lincolnton, North Carolina (d. 1912)
Thomas Neill Cream, Scottish-Canadian serial killer (Lambeth poisoner), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1892)
Billy Barnes, English cricket all-rounder (21 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 134, 51 wickets, BB 6/28; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Sutton-in-Ashfield, England (d. 1899)
Georges Eekhoud, Belgian writer (Jeune Belgium), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1927)
Manuel Teixeira Gomes, Portuguese politician and writer (7th President of Portugal 1923-25), born in Portimão, Portugal (d. 1941)
Victoria Earle Matthews, American author and activist, born in Fort Valley, Georgia (d. 1907)
Arthur Mold, English cricket fast bowler (England 3 Tests; 1,673 1st class wickets), born in Middleton Cheney, Northamptonshire (d. 1921)
Charles Wright, English cricket wicketkeeper (3 Tests, 1 x 50; Cambridge Uni CC, Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Harewood, Yorkshire, England (d. 1936)
Ante Trumbić, Croatian politician (23rd Mayor of Split), born in Split, Dalmatia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1938)
Arnold Bennett, English novelist (Anna of the Five Towns), born in Hanley, Staffordshire, England (d. 1931)
Lionel Palairet, British cricket batsman (2 Tests; Somerset CCC, Oxford Uni CC), born in Grange-over-Sands, Lancashire, England (d. 1933)
Georges Rouault, French expressionist painter (Miserere et Guerre), born in Paris (d. 1958)
Dustin Farnum, American actor (Squaw Man, Virginian, The Flaming Frontier), born in Hampton Beach, New Hampshire (d. 1929)
Richard von Schaukal, Moravian-Austria poet and writer (Eros Thanatos), born in Brno, Czech Republic (d. 1942)
Ferdynand Antoni Ossendowski, Polish writer (Lenin: God of the Godless), born in Ludza, Russian Empire (d. 1945)
Hans Lammers, German SS officer and Nazi politician, born in Lublinitz, Silesia, Prussia, German Empire (d. 1962)
Lucile Watson, Canadian actress (Great Lie, Watch on the Rhine, Let's Dance), born in Québec, Canada (d. 1962)
Rudolf Pannwitz, German writer and philosopher (Urblik), born in Crossen/Oder, Province of Brandenburg, Prussia (now in Poland) (d. 1969)
Max Brod, Czech-Israeli composer, writer, and biographer of Franz Kafka, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1968)
Emiel Delrue [Emiel van der Straeten], Belgian writer on colonial affairs (Sudanese Legends, The Southern Cross), born in Borgerhout, Belgium (d. 1918)[1]
Erich Kuttner, German journalist and historian (Vorwarts), born in Berlin (d. 1942)
Frank Woolley, English cricket all-rounder (64 Tests, 5 x 100, 23 x 50, HS 154, 83 wickets, BB 7/76; Kent CCC), born in Tonbridge, England (d. 1978)
Louis Durey, French composer (L'Offrande Lyrique), born in Paris (d. 1979)
Fritzi Brunette, American actress, born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 1943)
Claude Champagne, Canadian composer, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1965)
Sara Heyblom, Dutch actress (Pygmalion, Fietsen naar de Maan), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1990)
Hermann Dörnemann, German supercentarian, born in Essen, Germany (d. 2005)
Dashiell Hammett, American detective novelist (Sam Spade, Maltese Falcon), born in St. Mary's County, Maryland (d. 1961)
Louis-Ferdinand Céline, French writer (Die Nacht), born in Courbevoie, Seine, France (d. 1961)
Marij Kogoj, Slovenian composer, born in Trieste, Austria-Hungary (d. 1956)

John Cockcroft, English physicist (development of Radar, Nobel Prize 1951 for 1st particle accelerator, Director of Atomic Energy Research Establishment), born in Todmorden, Yorkshire, England (d. 1967)
Chester Zardis, American jazz 'slap-style' double-bassist, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1990)
Uładzimir Žyłka, Belarusian poet, born in Makaszy, Nesvizh, Russian Empire (d. 1933)
Conrad Elvehjem, American biochemist and nutrition scientist (discovered niacin), born in McFarland, Wisconsin (d. 1962)
Celius Dougherty, American pianist and composer, born in Glenwood, Minnesota (d. 1986)

Walt Kiesling, American Pro Football Hall of Fame guard (NFL 1920s All-Decade Team; First-team All-Pro 1929, 30, 32; Chicago Cardinals, Chicago Bears) and coach (Pittsburgh Pirates/Steelers), born in Saint Paul, Minnesota (d. 1962)
Chūhei Nambu, Japanese athlete (Olympic gold triple jump 1932; only person to have held WRs in both long jump & triple jump), born in Sapporo, Japan (d. 1997)
Robert Shone, British economist and director-general (NEDC), born in Birkenhead, Cheshire (d. 1992)
Felix de Nobel, Dutch orchestra leader and pianist, born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1981)
Alec Brown, English snooker player (Daily Mail Gold Cup 1939, 40; News of the World 1951), born in London (d. 1995)
Harold Rome, American composer (Fanny; Pins and Needles), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1993)
Melle Oldeboerrigter, Dutch painter and cartoonist, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1976)
Isador Goodman, South African-Australian pianist and composer (New Guinea Fantasy), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1982)
(Earl) "Jock" Caruthers American jazz saxophonist (Jimmie Lunceford Orchestra, 1932-47), born in Monroe, Mississippi (d. 1971)
Teddy Kollek, Israeli politician (Mayor of Jerusalem), born in Nagyvázsony, Hungary (d. 2007)
Torolf Elster, Norwegian journalist and writer (Frihet och demokrati), born in Kristiania, Norway (d. 2006)
Vincent Price, American actor (House on Haunted Hill, Fly, Laura), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1993)
John Cheever, American writer (Wapshot Chronicle, Pulitzer), born in Quincy, Massachusetts (d. 1982)
Rose Girone, Austrian-Polish supercentenarian (113 years, 42 days) and believed oldest living Holocaust survivor at her time of death, born in Janów, Austrian Poland (d. 2025)
Terry Moore, American baseball outfielder (World Series 1942, 46; 4 x MLB All Star; St. Louis Cardinals) and manager (Philadelphia Phillies 1954), born in Vernon, Alabama (d. 1995)
Arthur Mervyn Stockwood, Anglican bishop (Southwark England), born in Bridgend, Glamorgan, Wales (d. 1995)
Wols [Alfred Otto Wolfgang Schulze], German cartoonist and painter, born in Berlin (d. 1951)
Frederick Erroll, 1st Baron Erroll of Hale, British Conservative politician, born in London (d. 2000)
Hugh Le Caine, Canadian physicist and composer, born in Port Arthur, Ontario (d. 1977)
Ross Stainton, CEO (British Airways), born in Whitstable, England (d. 2011)
Herman Wouk, American novelist (The Caine Mutiny, Winds of War), born in New York City (d. 2019)
Bernard Oliver, American engineer at HP and inventor (first hand-held calculators, SETI, Pulse Code Modulation), born in Soquel, California (d. 1995)[1]
Kam Fong Chun, American actor (Hawaii Five-0, Magnum, P.I.), born in Kalihi, Territory of Hawaii (d. 2002)
Yasuhiro Nakasone, Japanese politician (Prime Minister of Japan 1982-87), born in Takasaki, Japan (d. 2019)
Gwyneth George, Welsh cellist and teacher, born on the Mumbles, Wales (d. 2016)
Caryl Chessman, American kidnapper and rapist who got the death penalty, born in St. Joseph, Michigan (d. 1960)
Sidney Keyes, English poet of World War II (Iron Laurel), born in Dartford, England (d. 1943)
Bernard Freyberg, British-New Zealand army officer (Victoria Cross, Governor General of New Zealand 1946-52), born in London, England (d. 1963)
Ingeborg "Inge" Morath, Austrian-born Magnum photographer and wife of Arthur Miller, born in Graz Austria (d. 2002)
Sumner Redstone, American media mogul (ViacomCBS), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2020)
Tony Hillerman, American writer (Navajo Tribal Police), born in Sacred Heart Oklahoma (d. 2008)
Vere Harmsworth, 3rd Viscount Rothermere, English press magnate (The Mail on Sunday), born in London, England (d. 1998)
(Clifford) “Bud” Shank, American jazz saxophonist and flute player, born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 2009)
Gordon Leggat, New Zealand cricket batsman (9 Tests, 2 x 50; Canterbury), born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 1973)
Kees Rijvers, Dutch soccer midfielder (33 caps; NAC Breda, Saint-Étienne, Feyenoord) and manager (FC Twente, PSV Eindhoven, Netherlands), born in Breda, Netherlands (d. 2024)
Bryan Cowgill, British broadcasting executive (BBC, 1963-77; Thames Television, 1977-85), born in Clitheroe, Lancashire, England (d. 2008)
Jan Blokker, Dutch journalist, editor (VPRO; de Volkskrant), novelist, and screenwriter, born in Admiralenbuurt, Netherlands (d. 2010)
Thea Musgrave, Scottish composer (Mary Queen of Scots; Pontalba), born in Barnton, Edinburgh, Scotland
Bjarne Hansen, Norwegian soccer centre-back (3 caps; Vålerenga FC), born in Oslo, Norway (d. 2023)
Eino Tamberg, Estonian composer (Joanna tentata), born in Tallinn, Estonia (d. 2010)
John Barth, American satirist and author (The Sot-Weed Factor; Lost in the Funhouse), and educator (Johns Hopkins University, 1973-91), born in Cambridge, Maryland (d. 2024)
William S. Sessions, American director of FBI (1987-93), born in Fort Smith, Arkansas (d. 2020)
James Wilson, American criminologist ('Zero Tolerance' policing), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2012)
John Chapple, British army officer (Chief of General Staff, 1988-92), born in Maida Vale, London, England (d. 2022)
Kenny Price, American country singer ("Walking on New Grass"; "Happy Tracks"), born in Florence, Kentucky (d. 1987)
Philip Kotler,  American economist, educator, and author considered the "Father of Modern Marketing", born in Chicago, Illinois
Věroslav Neumann, Czech composer and music educator, born in Citoliby, Czechoslovakia (d. 2006)
Jeffrey Bernard, British writer (Spectator Magazine - Low Life), born in Hampstead, london (d. 1997)
(Edward) "Ted" Rogers, Canadian entrepreneur (Rogers Communications), MLB team owner (Toronto Blue Jays), and philanthropist, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2008)
Harlan Ellison, American sci-fi author and screenwriter (Star Trek -"The City on the Edge of Forever"), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2018)
Elias Gistelinck, Flemish classical trumpeter and composer, born in Beveren Leie, Belgium (d. 2005)
Lee Meriwether, Miss America (Time Tunnel, Barnaby Jones), born in Los Angeles, California
